title: Explainers: immortal-time bias, lead-time bias, competing risks
context: Three specific biases routinely confound the interpretation of oncology observational studies and some trial analyses: immortal-time bias (patients must survive to receive treatment, so treatment arms appear to have better survival even if treatment is neutral); lead-time bias (earlier detection makes survival from diagnosis look longer even if death occurs at the same calendar time); and competing risks (a patient who dies of cardiac disease cannot die of cancer, so standard survival analysis overstates cancer-specific survival in the presence of competing causes). These are M2 advanced topics that build on km-006 and logrank-cox-011.
objective: Author, biostatistician-sign-off, and publish explainers on immortal-time bias, lead-time bias, and competing risks — covering the mechanism of each bias, the study designs or analyses where it typically arises, how to detect it, and the correct analytical approach — with reproducible worked examples on open/synthetic data.
acceptanceCriteria: Immortal-time bias explainer: plain and precise definition of immortal time; explicit commonTrap covering how it inflates apparent treatment benefit; description of how to recognize it in a study design (time-dependent exposure analysis); links to standard methods for correction.

Lead-time bias explainer: plain and precise definition; commonTrap covering why earlier detection can appear to lengthen survival without actually extending life; distinction between lead-time-adjusted and unadjusted survival estimates.

Competing risks explainer: plain and precise definition of competing risks; commonTrap covering how standard Kaplan-Meier analysis overestimates cumulative incidence in the presence of competing events (1 minus KM estimator bias); introduction of the cause-specific hazard and cumulative incidence function as alternatives.
Each bias has a worked example on open/synthetic data with a manifest entry; all figures and numbers emitted by committed scripts and diff-matched by repro-005.
Every conceptual claim cites an authoritative methods source; howToReadInAPaper checklists included for each bias.
Structural 'educational, not clinical advice' framing present; no specific trial verdict stated.
Biostatistician statsSignoff recorded; clinical-framing and accessibility reviews passed.
Zero stated statistical falsehoods; pnpm build && pnpm test && pnpm lint pass; DCO signed-off.
resources: Methods literature on immortal-time bias (Suissa), lead-time bias, competing risks (Fine & Gray, Geskus) — cited per-claim
output: Published, biostatistician-signed-off explainers on immortal-time bias, lead-time bias, and competing risks, with 100% reproducible worked examples, full provenance, all claims cited, accessibility passed, and structural 'not clinical advice' frame.
